answer:
'Lehd-i-be-naam' (لحد بے نام) — literally 'the nameless grave-niche' — is a recurring trope in Urdu ghazal that figures the obscure, unmarked burial of the lover: dying without fame, without mourners, without inscription, and without any lasting trace in the world. 'Lehd' (لحد) is the lateral niche cut into the side of a grave in which the body is placed; 'be-naam' (بے نام) means 'without a name / nameless.' Together the compound stages the lover's total social erasure: a death so anonymous that the earth itself leaves no record of it. The trope works in several registers: (i) melancholic irony — the lover who spent his life burning for another dies in complete obscurity; (ii) countercultural pride — the unmarked grave becomes a badge of authentic, socially-unrecognised suffering; (iii) Sufi-inflected self-annihilation — the fana of the mystic lover is so complete that no worldly name survives.

The strongest couplet thematising this in the corpus is Ghalib's ninth sher from the ghazal 'Yeh na thi hamari qismat ke visaal-e-yaar hota' (Diwan-e-Ghalib, chapter 204, paragraph p-e2bd26):

Original: ہوئے مر کے ہم جو رسوا ہوئے کیوں نہ غرقِ دریا / نہ کبھی جنازہ اٹھتا نہ کہیں مزار ہوتا
Transliteration: Huye mar ke hum jo ruswa, huye kyun na ghark-e-dariya / Na kabhi janaza uthta, na kahin mazaar hota
Translation (corpus): 'Having died and become disgraced, why did we not drown in the river? / Then no funeral procession would ever have been lifted, no grave would exist anywhere.'

The couplet is a direct, bitter meditation on the unmarked-grave trope: the speaker wishes he had drowned rather than died in disgrace with a marked grave. 'Na kahin mazaar hota' (no tomb anywhere) is the precise inversion of the 'lehd-i-be-naam' image — not merely a nameless grave but no grave at all, the ultimate dissolution of the self into anonymity.

A secondary example from Zauq (Diwan-e-Zauq, chapter 4) adds the same motif from the other direction: 'Nahin paayega nishaan koi hamara hargiz / Hum jahaan se ravish-e-teer-nazar jaayenge' ('No one will ever find a trace of us / we will depart from this world swift as a glance's flight') — the same erasure of all memorial trace.
